coredata.dc:title: Highly fluorescent dihydropyrimido-diindole derivative as a probe for detecting nitrite in food products and cell-imaging
coredata.dc:creator: He, Lingzhi
Zou, Wu
Zeng, Dan
Gong, Fuchun
Wang, Qinge
Xia, Jiaoyun
Cao, Zhong
coredata.dc:description: One-pot reaction approach to the synthesis of dihydropyrimido-diindole fluorophores (DPDI) was developed. In DPDI molecules, the two pyrrole rings in dipyrromethane were bridged by a carbon atom, eliciting a six-membered ring and rigid planar structure. Photophysical measurements demonstrated that DPDI can exhibit strong fluorescence, large Stokes shift and low sensitivity to pH. Cyclic voltamogram indicated a pair of redox peaks of DPDI near ±0.48V, implying that DPDI can be oxidized by nitrite (NO2 −). In the presence of NO2 −, DPDI showed apparent change in colours and fluorescence intensity owing to the implantation of NO2 −. From this basis, a highly sensitive and selective analysis of NO2 − was established using DPDI as probes, which offered a sensitive determination of NO2 − in the concentration range of 0–17 μM with the detection limit of 2.2 nM. Furthermore, DPDI was employed for fluorescence imaging, demonstrating the ability to discriminate from different concentrations of NO2 − in living cells. The newly developed DPDI dyes showed the obvious advantages of facile preparation and large Stokes shift over conventional dipyrrole-based phosphors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dihydropyrimido-diindole
Carbon-bridged dipyrromethane
Synthesis
Nitrite sensing
Bioimaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Cytotoxicity assays and fluorescence imaging using DPDI
Synthesis and characterization of DPDI dyes
Spectroscopic properties of DPDI and response to nitrite
Selectivity of DPDI for nitrite detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Absorption spectra of DPDI and the DPDI with the addition NO2 − in PBS buffer solutions (pH 6.5); (b) Fluorescence emission and excitation spectra of DPDI in a PBS buffer solution; (c) Fluorescence emission spectra of DPDI and the DPDI with the addition of NO2 − in a PBS buffer solution (pH 6.5); (d) The digital pictures of DPDI and the DPDI with the addition NO2 − under sunlight and UV light illumination, respectively. The concentrations of DPDI and NO2 − were 15 and 5 μM/L, respectively.

Cyclic voltammogram of 5 μM/L DPDI in dichloromethane containing tetra-n butylammonium bromide (TBAB, 5.5%). The three electrode system (glassy carbon electrode, silver electrode and calomel electrode) was used. Scan rate: 50 mV s −1.

(a) Selectivity of the probes DPDI to NO2 − over the interferent species; (b) Response profiles of DPDI to NO2 − in the presence of different interfering materials. The concentrations: NO2 −, 5.0 μM/L; the others, 15 μM/L; DPDI concentration was 10 μM/L in PBS (20 mM, pH6.5). The incubation temperature was at room temperature, and incubation time was 10 min. The excitation and emission is at 360 and 482 nm, respectively. The slit widths of emission and excitation were 5 nm.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of DPDI in the presence of different concentrations NO2 −, including 0, 1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, 13, 15 and 17 μM (from top to bottom, respectively) with excitation at 360 nm; (b) The linear relationship between F/F
0 and NO2 − concentrations in the range of 1–17 μM.

Confocal microscopic images of pancreatic cancer cells incubated with the DPDI probe (b) and the pancreatic cancer cells with the pretreatment of 2.5 (d) and 5.0 μM NO2 − (f) before incubation with the DPDI. (a), (c) and (e) are the bright fields of (b), (d) and (f), respectively.
Synthesis of DPDI and the possible mechanism of DPDI to sense nitrite.
Results of the determination of nitrite in different food samples (n = 3, mean ± SD).

head.ce:title: Highly fluorescent dihydropyrimido-diindole derivative as a probe for detecting nitrite in food products and cell-imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Food Engineering, Changsha University of Science and Technology, Changsha, 410114, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Food Engineering
Changsha University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hunan Key Laboratory of Cell Chemistry, Changsha University of Science and Technology, Changsha, 410114, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hunan Key Laboratory of Cell Chemistry
Changsha University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: . Hunan Provincial Key Laboratory of Materials Protection for Electric Power and Transportation, Changsha University of Science and Technology, Changsha, 410114, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Hunan Key Laboratory of Cell Chemistry, Changsha University of Science and Technology, Changsha, 410114, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hunan Key Laboratory of Cell Chemistry
Changsha University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: One-pot reaction approach to the synthesis of dihydropyrimido-diindole fluorophores (DPDI) was developed. In DPDI molecules, the two pyrrole rings in dipyrromethane were bridged by a carbon atom, eliciting a six-membered ring and rigid planar structure. Photophysical measurements demonstrated that DPDI can exhibit strong fluorescence, large Stokes shift and low sensitivity to pH. Cyclic voltamogram indicated a pair of redox peaks of DPDI near ±0.48V, implying that DPDI can be oxidized by nitrite (NO2 −). In the presence of NO2 −, DPDI showed apparent change in colours and fluorescence intensity owing to the implantation of NO2 −. From this basis, a highly sensitive and selective analysis of NO2 − was established using DPDI as probes, which offered a sensitive determination of NO2 − in the concentration range of 0–17 μM with the detection limit of 2.2 nM. Furthermore, DPDI was employed for fluorescence imaging, demonstrating the ability to discriminate from different concentrations of NO2 − in living cells. The newly developed DPDI dyes showed the obvious advantages of facile preparation and large Stokes shift over conventional dipyrrole-based phosphors.

ce:section.ce:para: Nitrite, an important intermediate in the biological nitrogen cycle, exists widely in the food products, water and environment. To suppress the propagation of Clostridium botulinum and improve the flavor of meat, it has been used as a food additive in meat preservation and processing [1,2]. However, excessive amount of nitrite in food or water is highly harmful, especially for pregnant women and infants. For instance, it may interfere with the body's oxygen delivery biotissue, decreasing the capacity of hemoglobin to carry oxygen [3,4]. The excess concentration of nitrite can also react with the secondary amines and amides in foods to form carcinogenic N-nitroso compounds, which are known to be carcinogenic [5,6]. The limit of nitrite in potable water is 60 μg/L and the acceptable daily intake of nitrite is less than 0.07 mg/kg body weight (the US Public Health Association) [7]. Therefore, the simple, selective and sensitive methods are required to provide a way for tracking the legal recommended limits of nitrite in agricultural samples, water and foods.

Over the last decade, a great effort has devoted to develop analytical procedures for rapid nitrite determination based on separation techniques, including gas chromatography [8,9], high performance liquid chromatography [10–12], capillary electrophoresis [13], flow injection analysis [14,15] and ion chromatography [16]. By improving the extraction procedures, great progress arose, which leaded to the decreased interference in samples and helped to lower the detection limits of the analytes [17,18]. The major advantages of the separation-based methods are their relatively satisfactory separation of the nitrite from interfering compounds, analysis speed and low detection limits, as well as the complexity of the matrix, which may require tedious sample pretreatment steps [19,20]. Although ultraviolet–visible spectral and electrochemical methods are also available for the determination of nitrite, their sensitivity and selectivity often fail to meet practical requirements [21,22].

More recently, fluorescent probe methods have received immensely attention because of their high sensitivity, excellent selectivity, fast response, and simple operation. Numerous fluorescent probes for nitrite detection have been designed and synthesized. Among them, the fluorophores containing aromatic o-diamines were frequently employed as reactive probes, which can react with NO+ (derived from nitrite in an acidic resolution) to give fluorescent benzotriazole species [23,24]. For these probes, the wanted “off–on” switch in fluorescence intensity was acquired through the disturbance of photoinduced electron transfer (PET) mechanism [25,26]. However, these techniques accompanied by inherent pitfalls. Such fluorescent probes generally suffered the possible interference from potentially competing species in analytical samples, such as ascorbic acid (AA) and oxidizers in biosamples [27–29]. Some aromatic o-diamine substrates (probes) and/or the resulted products benzotriazole were popularly pH-sensitive, and their protonation under acidic media may bring the false response [30–32]. In addition, most of developed o-diamine based probes often suffer from the long time for response to analytes [33,34] and/or tedious synthetic steps [35,36]. Therefore, the development of highly selective, fast responding and easily synthesized fluorescent probes for convenience measurement of nitrite are very necessary.

Herein, we reported a robust and convenient methodology for the synthesis of a new class of fluorophores dihydropyrimido-diindole (DPDI). The suggested approach enables one to obtain different dipyrromethane-based dyes by varying the substituents in both pyrroles and the terminal benzene rings of DPDI scaffolds. The resultant DPDI can emit strong blue-green fluorescence; the addition of nitrite results in significant fluorescence quenching. On this basis, the probe DPDI was used to detect nitrite through fluorescence “turn-off” mechanism with the detection limit of 2.2 nM. The high efficiency in the preparation of DPDI is rather remarkable, with one-pot reaction, no additional catalyst needed.

The fluorescence spectra were recorded on a F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). UV–vis absorption spectra were measured using a Cary 60 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, Australia). 1HNMR spectra were recorded using a Bruker AVB-400 MHz NMR spectrometer (Bruker biospin, Switzerland). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra in KBr were collected on a WQF-510 FT-IR spectrometer (Beijing Rayleigh Analytical Instrument Co., Ltd., Beijing, China).

All chemicals and solvents were purchased commercially and used as received without further purification. Double-distilled water was used for the preparation of all solutions. 3-methylindole, p-methoxybenzaldehyde, trichloroacetic acid and sodium nitrite (NaNO2) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The stock solution of 5.0 mM/L NO2 − was prepared by dissolving sodium nitrite in PBS buffer (20 mM, pH6.5). The stock solution of DPDI (15 mM/L) was also prepared in the same PBS buffer. Stock solution of interfering material (1.5 mM) was prepared by dissolving their inorganic salt in PBS buffer (20 mM, pH6.5) and stored at 4 °C until use. Working solutions were prepared by diluting the corresponding stock solutions to an appropriate volume with PBS buffer (20 mM, pH6.5) whenever required. The human pancreatic cancer cell lines were purchased from Shanghai Institutes for Biological Sciences (Shanghai, China).

An appropriate amount of 3-methylindole (100 mmol) and aldehydes (50 mmol) were dissolved in propionic acid (30 ml), then trichloroacetic acid (55 mmol) was added into the mixture. After reaction at 140 °C for 4 h, the contents were cooled to room temperature and solvent evaporated under reduced pressure to give a solid mass, which was chromatographed over silica gel. The desired compound was eluted using a mixture of dichloromethane/ethyl acetate (8:2). The fractions containing 3 were combined, solvent evaporated, and the product crystallized from methanol in every case. Obtained as brown solid; yield: 1.27 g, 66%; m.p.: 214–216 °C. 1HNMR (600 MHz, DMSO) δ: 11.47(s,1H), 7.54(m, 2H), 7.51(m, 2H), 7.37(m, 2H), 7.12(m, 2H), 7.00(m, 2H), 6.83(m, 2H), 5.61(s,1H), 3.83(s,3H), (s, 6H,-CH3); 13CNMR (DMSO) δ: 175.69, 157.59, 140.21, 137.41, 135.26, 130.02, 129.41, 119.94, 113.35, 109.14, 106.40, 54.98, 39.50, 9.55; IR (KBr) ν/cm−1: 3407, 2977, 2922, 1623, 1509, 1454, 1372, 1344, 1231, 1166, 1049, 823, 747, 497; EI-MS, m/z: 470.57(M+); Anal. calcd. for C28H23N2O3Cl: C 71.41, H 4.89, N 5.95, O 10.20,Cl 7.54. Found: C 71.52, H 4.92, N 6.08, Cl 7.62%.

All the food products were obtained from the local supermarket in Changsha (China), and the meat samples (spiced beef and smoked bacon) were triturated. For the sample preparation, the samples (12 g, three parallel samples) were weighed and put into in a beaker, 15 mL Na2B4O7.10H2O solution (4.5%) and 250 mL hot water were then added. Next, the resulting mixture was placed in a hot water bath (80–90 °C) for a 15 min-extraction followed by cooling down to room temperature. The incubated mixture was then transferred to a volumetric flask, and 10 mL K4[Fe(CN)6]·3H2O solution (15%) and a 10 mL C4H6O4Zn aqueous solution (10%) was added to the precipitate mass. Finally, the volume was adjusted to 500 mL followed by filtration through the filter paper (35 cm), and the filtrate was collected and stored at 4 °C.

The vegetable samples (Chinese leaves and radish peel) were cleaned with pure water and dried with a blower, followed by crushing the dried samples before use. The test vegetable samples were then prepared by the similar aforementioned procedures for meat samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity assays and fluorescence imaging using DPDI
ce:section.ce:para: Cytotoxicity assays: Pancreatic cancer cells were employed as model cell lines to evaluate the in vitro cytotoxicity of DPDI using the MTT assay. Firstly, pancreatic cancer cells were incubated in a 96-well plate (5000 cells/well), and added with a 100 μL DMEM solution containing 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) under humidified 4% CO2 atmosphere at 37 °C. After one-day incubation, the culture medium was replaced and added various concentrations of DPDI for another day-incubation. Next, 25 μL test solution containing 10 mg/mL MTT reagent was injected into each well, further incubated at 37 °C for additional 4 h. Finally, the medium mass was removed and 150 μL DMSO was then added and shaken for 10 min in the dark. The resulting mixtures were used for UV–Vis spectrometry by a microplate reader at the wavelength of 490 nm.

Fluorescence imaging of living cells: The distribution of DPDI in pancreatic cancer cells and the fluorescence imaging of intracellular nitrite were also investigated using confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM). Typically, pancreatic cancer cells were placed in a 45 mm glass-bottom culture dish at the density of 1 × 105 cells per dish, making it grow overnight in a 2.5 mL DMEM with 10% FBS with 5% CO2 humidified atmosphere at 37 °C for a 24 h-incubation. After that, the culture medium was eliminated and a 2 mL fresh medium solution containing 0.2 mM/L DPDI was then added into each dish. After a 12 h-incubation, ice-cold PBS buffer (20 mM, pH 7.4) was added to wash the remaining DPDI. The resulting cells were then treated with different concentrations of nitrite (0, 2.5 and 5 μM) for 1 h-incubation and incubated in a fresh medium containing DPDI (1.5
μ
M/L) for 4 h. After rinsing with an ice-cold PBS buffer for three times, the resulting cells were observed under CLSM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of DPDI dyes
ce:section.ce:para: As illustrated in Scheme 1 a, 6-chloro-13-(4-methoxyphenyl)-12,14-dimethyl-6,13-dihydropyrimido[1,6-a:3,4-a']diindole-6-carboxylic acid (DPDI) was prepared using trichloroacetic acid as a catalyst in propionic acid at 140 °C via one-pot A3-coupling reaction. Briefly, 3-methylindole (1.31 g, 10 mM), 4-methoxybenzaldehyde (1.36 g, 10 mM) and, trichloroacetic acid (1.80 g, 11 mM) was dissolved in 25 mL propionic acid. After reaction at 140 °C for 4 h, the contents were then cooled to room temperature and the solvent evaporated under reduced pressure to give a solid mass, which was chromatographed over silica gel. The desired compound was eluted using a mixture of dichloromethane/ethyl acetate (8:2). The synthesized compound was characterized by FTIR, NMR and mass spectral analysis. The details of experimental procedure with complete characterization data were given in the experimental section.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spectroscopic properties of DPDI and response to nitrite
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of DPDI and their response to nitrite were investigated using UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy. The absorption spectrum of DPDI in ethanol-water system (1.0:1.0, v/v) shows three clear peaks at 265, 340 and 420 nm, respectively (Fig. 1 a). The strong peak at 265 nm was related to the π-π* transitions in the carbon core of the aromatic π-bonded system [37]. The peaks at 340 and 420 nm were pointed to the n-π* transition of the C–N bonds and their aromatic rings [38]. Similarly, three peaks at 265, 340 nm and 417 nm were observed in the fluorescence excitation spectrum of DPDI (Fig. 1b) that can be attributed to the σ-π* and π-π* transitions. When excited with a 360 nm UV lamp, DPDI showed strong blue-green luminescence (Fig. 1d). The absolute quantum yield was calculated to be 49.4% using the absolute method as previously reported [39], which is much higher than that of dipyrrole-based phosphors [40].

Then, the absorption and fluorescence response of DPDI to nitrite were researched. As shown in Fig. 1a, DPDI exhibited strong absorption at 420 nm in the absence of nitrite. However, upon the addition of nitrite (0.01, 0.03 μM), the absorption of DPDI at 420 nm were significantly lowered. Similarly, the addition of nitrite elicited definite change in fluorescence intensity and colours of DPDI (Fig. 1c and d), which indicated the valuable response of DPDI for detecting nitrite. The possible mechanism for the fluorescence quenching involves the formation of nitroso-DPDI (Scheme1b).

The redox properties of DPDI were investigated by cyclic voltammetry (CV). The CV data is suggestive of one oxidation and reduction processes in the DPDI dyes within the electrochemical window. Under typical conditions used for CV, the substituted DPDI dyes showed a reversible redox wave with the onset potential of Eox/onset = ±2.0 V (Fig. 2 ). The oxidation (0.48 V) was assigned to the oxidation of the electron-donating methoxyphenyl fragment of DPDI, while the reduction processes in DPDI (−0.48 V) was assigned to the reduction of the DPDI core in these D-A systems. This oxidation potential is lower than that of nitrite (0.98 V), implying that nitrite can react with DPDI.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of DPDI for nitrite detection
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence screening experiments were carried out for determining nitrite and identifying potential interfering substances (Na+, Co2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Fe3+, F−, Cl−, Br−, P2O7 4−, HPO4 −, Ac−, SO3 2−, HSO4 −, CO3 2−, HCO3 −, NO3 −, ATP and H2O2) within DPDI solution. Nineteen kinds of biologically relevant ions, ATP and H2O2 were used as interfering species at a concentration of 15 μM/L (similar to the case of NO2 − excited at 360 nm and reacted for time 10 min) to estimate the changes in fluorescence intensity before and after the addition. As shown in Fig. 3 a, only the NO2 − ions resulted in obvious fluorescence quenching of DPDI at 482 nm (quenching 78% of base case at 482 nm); almost no changes in fluorescence intensity were observed in remaining ions and compounds.

In order to evaluate the anti-interference ability of DPDI for detecting NO2 −, we also performed several control experiments by adding potential interfering substances into the tested NO2 − solutions. The results showed that negligible change in fluorescence intensity even if the excesses of the interfering metal ions coexisted, anion ions or biologically related molecules (Fig. 3b).Therefore, these results demonstrate that DPDI possess strong anti-interference ability. The mild quenching may attributed to interaction between NO2 − and methoxyphenyl moieties of DPDI [41,42]. These results indicate the high selectivity of DPDI to NO2 −.

To get better sensing performance of DPDI for nitrite detection, it is necessary to optimize the conditions which may significantly affect the detection of nitrite, including the pH value of medium solutions, fluorescence quenching time and reaction temperature. Usually, the pH of medium has a strong impact on the fluorescence behavior of organic phosphors and the reactivity of nitrite, therefore we have investigated the effect of pH value ranging from 3.0 to 11.0 on the fluorescence of DPDI. As shown in Fig. S3a, the fluorescence intensity of the DPDI showed only a slight decrease as the http://www.baidu.com/link?url=dJs6Ad242TfxrM_TfLzUmhqv8GOX-bhw7jsM78-N-K7vSgYoQcJPcOj9aC3EEQzsXb7SHEJWM-xgsAqYZw3yBgLeec1wzu7ePr7EE58_jxqpH value increasing from 4.5 to 9.0. When the pH value is less than 4.5, the fluorescence of DPDI decreased significantly. On the other hands, the higher pH will suppress the reactivity of nitrite. When the pH value is higher than 9.0, nitrite will lose its reactivity. Therefore, we chose 6.5 as the pH value to measure the fluorescence response of DPDI to nitrite on the comprehensive consideration.

At the relatively low concentrations, where the fluorescence measurements were performed, the reaction of DPDI with nitrite was found to be time- and temperature-dependent. Hence, the completion of the reaction was obtained by putting the test tube in a hot water bath. The impact of time and temperature on the reaction of DPDI with nitrite were outlined in Figure S3b, c. It was found that a higher temperature was necessary to obtain obvious fluorescence quenching. But when the temperature was raised from 20 to 60 °C, the fluorescence quenching was almost constant (Fig. S3c). At 20–60 °C, the fluorescence quenching was basically stable after 10 min of reaction. Therefore, the optimized conditions for the analyzing process were found to be at 35 °C for 5 min.

Under the optimized conditions, the fluorescence spectra of DPDI in the absence and presence of nitrite were recorded in the concentrations ranging from 0 to 17 μM L−1 (Fig. 4 a). The fluorescence quenching was linear over this nitrite concentration range. From this basis, a linear calibration curve could be constructed ranging from 0 to 17 μmol L−1 (Fig. 4b). The concentration of nitrite could be calculated from the linear regression equation: ΔF = −295.16 c + 4270.80 (R2 = 0.9975), where “ΔF” is the fluorescence quenching and “c” is the concentration of the added nitrite. The relative standard deviation (RSD) for six replicate determinations is 2.3% for 3 μM nitrite. The calculated limit detection is 2.2 nM (S/N = 3), which is far below the maximum contaminant nitrite level in drinking water (1 ppm) [43]. Additionally, compared with some spectrofluorimetric methods and fluorescent probes, our strategy using DPDI for detecting nitrite is better than that of many related nitrite assays [44,45]. The above analysis results demonstrating the potential of the DPDI could be used as a fluorescent probe for the determination of nitrite.

The practicability of the DPDI probes was then examined by the quantitative detection of nitrite in food samples. DPDI was used to monitor nitrite levels in meat products, vegetables and water samples. As shown in Table 1 , the determination results showed a good agreement with those obtained by standard methods based on GB 5009.33.2010 in literature [46]. The recoveries of the spiked samples ranged from 97.6% to 104.1%, displaying the feasible application ability of the proposed probes. These results suggest that our developed strategy has potential for monitoring nitrite in food samples.

To examine the application of DPDI for fluorescence imaging in living cells, DPDI (1.0 μM) was added into the culture medium containing pancreatic cancer cells and stained for 30 min at 37 °C. After washing with PBS buffer (20 mM, pH 7.4), the resulting cells were used for CLSM. As shown in Fig. 5 , under the excitation wavelength of 360 nm, the pancreatic cancer cells stained with 5 μM DPDI for 30 min at 37 °C showed strong blue-green fluorescence (Fig. 5b). When the cells were pretreated with NO2 − of 2.5 and 5.0 μM, respectively, the cells incubated in the same DPDI solution could exhibit weak fluorescence (Fig. 5d, f), indicating the ability to distinguish different concentrations of NO2 −. In addition, it can be observed that DPDI has good cell-permeability and is mainly concentrated in the cytoplasm of cells. These results show the good cell-membrane permeability of DPDI, and it can thus be used to track nitrite and discriminate its concentrations in living cells.

One-pot reaction approach to the synthesis of dihydropyrimido-diindole dyes (DPDI) was developed using trichloroacetic acid as a catalyst. Electrochemical performance of DPDI showed the ability of reaction with nitrite. DPDI solution exhibited strong blue-green fluorescence in a wide pH range. Upon the addition of nitrite, the fluorescence intensity of DPDI was significantly lowered. Among the selected biologically relevant substances, the probe DPDI was able to detect nitrite through fluorescence “turn-off” mechanism with the detection limit of 2.2 nM. The proposed method exhibited good selectivity, high sensitivity, and avoidance of coexisting substances. The detection limit of this newly developed method was lower or comparable to most of reported spectrofluorimetric methods. CLSM results also revealed the potential applications to cell-imaging in live cells. The real sample analyses displayed acceptable average recovery values. From these results it can be concluded that the probe DPDI is highly selective and sensitive towards detecting nitrite and cell-imaging.

Lingzhi He: Conceptualization, Investigation. Wu Zou: Data curation, Formal analysis. Dan Zeng: Methodology. Fuchun Gong: Funding acquisition, Writing - original draft. Qinge Wang: Resources, Software. Jiaoyun Xia: Writing - review & editing. Zhong Cao: Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that we have no conflict of interest. This article does not contain any studies with human or animal subjects.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 31527803), the Research Foundation of Education Bureau of Hunan Province (No.18A141), Hunan Natural Science Research Foundation (No. 2018JJ2422) and the Graduate Research and Innovation Foundation of Changsha University of Science and Technology (No. CX2019SS29).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2020.108256.

Dyes Pigments

sb:title.sb:maintitle: S-nitroso-N-acetylcysteine generates less carcinogenic N-nitrosamines in meat products than nitrite
J Agric Food Chem

Talanta
